1:
"Beloved, believe not every spirit, but try the spirits whether they are of God: because many false prophets are gone out into the world." The witnesses note the call to test the spirits, for many false prophets have gone out. On "believe not every spirit, but try the spirits whether they are of God," the grammatical voices note the duty of discernment. Barnes and Robertson note the warning: "believe not every spirit (every claimed inspiration or teacher), but try (dokimazete — test, prove, examine) the spirits whether they are of God" — believers are not to accept every teacher claiming the Spirit, but to test them; discernment is a Christian duty.

On "try the spirits," the witnesses note the testing. Barnes and Calvin note that "the spirits" are the teachers claiming to speak by the Spirit; they are to be tested by a standard (the confession of Christ, v.2); not all who claim inspiration are truly of God, so testing is necessary.

On "many false prophets are gone out into the world," the puritan Henry notes the reason: "many false prophets are gone out into the world" — the proliferation of false teachers is the reason testing is needed; the world is full of those who falsely claim to speak for God; their multitude demands vigilant discernment. The verse displays the call to test the spirits, for many false prophets have gone out.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the duty — "believe not every spirit, but try (test) the spirits whether they are of God," not accepting every claimed teacher but testing them, discernment a duty (Barnes, Robertson); "the spirits" the teachers claiming the Spirit, tested by a standard (Barnes, Calvin); "many false prophets are gone out into the world," the proliferation of false teachers the reason for testing (Henry). The verse displays the testing of spirits. The verse marks the warning: believe not every spirit. The verse displays the duty: try the spirits. The verse displays the standard: of God. The verse displays the reason: many false prophets. The call displays the believer testing every teacher. The verse displays the spirits: tested. The verse displays the false prophets: many. The verse displays the discernment: required. The call to test the spirits, for many false prophets have gone out, displays the duty of discernment: believers are not to believe every spirit but to test the spirits whether they are of God — for many false prophets have gone out into the world. The verse displays the testing of spirits, whose criterion is the confession of Christ (v.2).
2:
"Hereby know ye the Spirit of God: Every spirit that confesseth that Jesus Christ is come in the flesh is of God:" The witnesses note the test of the Spirit: confessing Christ come in the flesh. On "hereby know ye the Spirit of God," the grammatical voices note the criterion. Barnes and Robertson note the test given: "hereby (by this) know ye the Spirit of God" — John provides a definite criterion by which to distinguish the Spirit of God from false spirits; the test of true inspiration.

On "every spirit that confesseth that Jesus Christ is come in the flesh is of God," the witnesses note the confession. Barnes and Calvin note the standard: "every spirit that confesseth (acknowledges, openly professes) that Jesus Christ is come in the flesh (has truly come as a real man, incarnate) is of God" — the true test is the confession of the incarnation; the spirit that confesses Christ truly come in the flesh is of God.

On "Jesus Christ is come in the flesh," the grammatical-historical note the anti-docetic point: the test targets the early error (the docetists/gnostics) who denied that Christ truly came in real flesh; to confess "Jesus Christ come in the flesh" is to affirm the genuine incarnation against those who denied it; the confession of the true humanity (and the union of the divine Christ with it) is the mark of the Spirit of God. The verse displays the test of the Spirit: confessing Christ come in the flesh.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the criterion — "hereby know ye the Spirit of God," a definite test for distinguishing the true Spirit (Barnes, Robertson); "every spirit that confesseth that Jesus Christ is come in the flesh is of God," the confession of the incarnation the standard (Barnes, Calvin); the test targeting the docetists who denied Christ's true flesh, the confession of the genuine incarnation the mark (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the test. The verse marks the criterion: know the Spirit. The verse displays the confession: Christ come in the flesh. The verse displays the target: docetic denial. The verse displays the mark: the true incarnation. The test displays the Spirit known by confessing the incarnate Christ. The verse displays the Spirit: of God. The verse displays the confession: of the flesh. The verse displays the standard: the incarnation. The test of the Spirit — confessing Christ come in the flesh — displays the criterion of true inspiration: by this you know the Spirit of God: every spirit that confesses that Jesus Christ has truly come in the flesh is of God — the confession of the genuine incarnation, against those who denied it. The verse displays the confession of Christ, whose denial marks the spirit of antichrist (v.3).
3:
"And every spirit that confesseth not that Jesus Christ is come in the flesh is not of God: and this is that spirit of antichrist, whereof ye have heard that it should come; and even now already is it in the world." The witnesses note the spirit of antichrist that denies Christ come in the flesh. On "every spirit that confesseth not that Jesus Christ is come in the flesh is not of God," the grammatical voices note the negative test. Barnes and Robertson note the converse of v.2: "every spirit that confesseth not (denies, refuses to acknowledge) that Jesus Christ is come in the flesh is not of God" — the spirit that denies the incarnation is not from God; the refusal of this confession marks the false spirit.

On "this is that spirit of antichrist," the witnesses note the identification. Barnes and Calvin note that the spirit denying the incarnate Christ "is that spirit of antichrist (the antichristian spirit)" — this denial is the very spirit of antichrist, the opposition to Christ that John had warned of; to deny Christ come in the flesh is antichristian.

On "whereof ye have heard that it should come; and even now already is it in the world," the puritan Henry notes its presence: the spirit of antichrist, "whereof ye have heard that it should come," is "even now already in the world" — the antichristian spirit, foretold as coming, is already at work; not merely a future figure but a present reality in the false teachers. The verse displays the spirit of antichrist that denies Christ come in the flesh.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the negative test — "every spirit that confesseth not that Jesus Christ is come in the flesh is not of God," the denial of the incarnation marking the false spirit (Barnes, Robertson); "this is that spirit of antichrist," the denial the very antichristian spirit (Barnes, Calvin); "even now already is it in the world," the antichristian spirit foretold and already present in the false teachers (Henry). The verse displays the antichristian spirit. The verse marks the denial: not confessing Christ. The verse displays the identification: antichrist. The verse displays the warning: foretold to come. The verse displays the presence: already here. The teaching displays the denial of the incarnation as antichristian. The verse displays the spirit: of antichrist. The verse displays the denial: of the flesh. The verse displays the presence: in the world. The spirit of antichrist that denies Christ come in the flesh displays the negative side of the test: every spirit that does not confess Jesus Christ come in the flesh is not of God — this is the spirit of antichrist, foretold to come and already at work in the world. The verse displays the antichristian spirit, overcome by those who are of God (v.4).
4:
"Ye are of God, little children, and have overcome them: because greater is he that is in you, than he that is in the world." The witnesses note the believers' victory through the greater One within them. On "ye are of God, little children, and have overcome them," the grammatical voices note the assurance of victory. Barnes and Robertson note the encouragement: "ye are of God (belong to God), little children, and have overcome them (the false prophets, the antichristian spirits)" — the believers, belonging to God, have already overcome the false teachers; victory over the spirit of antichrist is theirs.

On "have overcome them," the latin-fathers Augustine notes the source of victory. Augustine and Barnes note that the believers' overcoming is not by their own strength but by God's power working in them; they conquer the false teachers' errors through the indwelling God.

On "greater is he that is in you, than he that is in the world," the grammatical-historical note the ground: "greater is he that is in you (God, the Holy Spirit, dwelling in the believer), than he that is in the world (the devil, the spirit of antichrist, working in the world)" — the reason for their victory is the superior power of the God within them over the evil one in the world; the indwelling God is greater than all opposing powers; the believer's confidence rests on this. The verse displays the believers' victory through the greater One within them.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the victory — "ye are of God, little children, and have overcome them (the false prophets)," the believers belonging to God and overcoming the antichristian spirits (Barnes, Robertson); the overcoming by God's power, not their own (Augustine, Barnes); "greater is he that is in you, than he that is in the world," the indwelling God greater than the devil in the world, the ground of victory (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the assured victory. The verse marks the belonging: of God. The verse displays the victory: overcome them. The verse displays the ground: the greater One within. The verse displays the contrast: God in you, the devil in the world. The assurance displays the believer conquering by the greater indwelling God. The verse displays the children: of God. The verse displays the overcoming: accomplished. The verse displays the greater: within. The believers' victory through the greater One within them displays the encouragement against the false teachers: you are of God and have overcome them, because greater is he who is in you than he who is in the world — the indwelling God overpowering the spirit of antichrist. The verse displays the victorious believers, distinguished from the world that hears the false teachers (v.5).
5:
"They are of the world: therefore speak they of the world, and the world heareth them." The brief verse notes the false teachers of the world, heard by the world. The grammatical voices note their worldly origin. Barnes and Robertson note the contrast with the believers (v.4): "they (the false teachers) are of the world (belong to the world, have its nature): therefore speak they of (from, according to) the world" — the false teachers belong to the world, and so their teaching is worldly in origin and character; they speak from the world's viewpoint.

On "speak they of the world," the witnesses note the worldly message. Barnes and Calvin note that because they are of the world, their message suits the world — its wisdom, its values, its desires; their teaching has nothing of God in it but reflects the world from which it springs.

On "the world heareth them," the eastern Cyril notes the kinship: "the world heareth (listens to, receives) them" — the world receives the false teachers because they are its own; like attracts like; the worldly gladly hear a worldly message; the reception of the false teachers by the world is itself a mark of their origin. The verse displays the false teachers of the world, heard by the world.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the worldly origin — "they are of the world: therefore speak they of (from) the world," the false teachers belonging to the world, their teaching worldly (Barnes, Robertson); their message suiting the world's wisdom and desires, nothing of God in it (Barnes, Calvin); "the world heareth them," the world receiving its own, like attracting like (Cyril). The verse displays the worldly teachers. The verse marks the origin: of the world. The verse displays the message: from the world. The verse displays the audience: the world. The verse displays the kinship: like to like. The teaching displays the false teachers and their hearers both of the world. The verse displays the teachers: worldly. The verse displays the speech: worldly. The verse displays the hearers: the world. The false teachers of the world, heard by the world, display the mark of their origin: they are of the world, so they speak from the world, and the world hears them — the worldly message gladly received by the worldly. The verse displays the world's teachers, over against those who hear the apostles (v.6).
6:
"We are of God: he that knoweth God heareth us; he that is not of God heareth not us. Hereby know we the spirit of truth, and the spirit of error." The witnesses note the test of hearing the apostles: the spirit of truth and of error. On "we are of God: he that knoweth God heareth us," the grammatical voices note the apostolic test. Barnes and Robertson note the contrast with the false teachers (v.5): "we (the apostles) are of God: he that knoweth God heareth (receives, listens to) us" — the apostles are of God, and the one who truly knows God receives their teaching; receptiveness to the apostolic message is a mark of being of God.

On "he that is not of God heareth not us," the witnesses note the converse. Barnes and Calvin note that "he that is not of God heareth not (rejects) us" — the one who does not belong to God rejects the apostolic teaching; rejection of the apostles' word marks the one not of God; the response to the apostolic message tests the hearer.

On "hereby know we the spirit of truth, and the spirit of error," the grammatical-historical note the criterion: "hereby (by this) know we the spirit of truth (the Holy Spirit, true teaching), and the spirit of error (false, deceiving spirit)" — by the response to the apostolic teaching, the two spirits are distinguished; reception of the apostolic gospel marks the spirit of truth, rejection the spirit of error. The verse displays the test of hearing the apostles: the spirit of truth and of error.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the apostolic test — "we are of God: he that knoweth God heareth us," the apostles of God, the one who knows God receiving their teaching (Barnes, Robertson); "he that is not of God heareth not us," the one not of God rejecting the apostolic word (Barnes, Calvin); "hereby know we the spirit of truth, and the spirit of error," the response to the apostles distinguishing the two spirits (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the test. The verse marks the apostles: of God. The verse displays the receptive: knows God. The verse displays the rejecter: not of God. The verse displays the criterion: truth and error. The test displays the spirits known by response to the apostolic word. The verse displays the apostles: heard. The verse displays the truth: received. The verse displays the error: rejecting. The test of hearing the apostles — the spirit of truth and of error — displays the criterion of discernment: we are of God; the one who knows God hears us, and the one not of God does not — and by this we know the spirit of truth and the spirit of error. The verse displays the spirit of truth, which is the love of God (v.7).
7:
"Beloved, let us love one another: for love is of God; and every one that loveth is born of God, and knoweth God." The witnesses note that love is of God, and the one who loves is born of God. On "let us love one another: for love is of God," the grammatical voices note the source of love. Barnes and Robertson note the exhortation grounded in love's origin: "let us love one another: for love is of God (springs from God, has God as its source)" — believers are to love one another because love itself comes from God; love is divine in origin, a reflection of God's own nature.

On "love is of God," the witnesses note the divine source. Barnes and Calvin note that "love is of God" — all true love originates in God; he is its fountain; to love is to share in something that comes from God himself; love is not merely human sentiment but divine in source.

On "every one that loveth is born of God, and knoweth God," the puritan Henry notes the evidence: "every one that loveth (with true Christian love) is born of God, and knoweth God" — genuine love is the proof of the new birth and of knowing God; the one who loves shows he is God's child and has true acquaintance with God; love is the mark of regeneration and of the knowledge of God. The verse displays that love is of God, and the one who loves is born of God.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the source — "let us love one another: for love is of God," believers to love because love comes from God, divine in origin (Barnes, Robertson); "love is of God," all true love originating in God, its fountain (Barnes, Calvin); "every one that loveth is born of God, and knoweth God," genuine love the proof of the new birth and of knowing God (Henry). The verse displays the divine love. The verse marks the exhortation: love one another. The verse displays the source: love is of God. The verse displays the evidence: born of God. The verse displays the knowledge: knoweth God. The teaching displays love as divine in source and the mark of regeneration. The verse displays the love: of God. The verse displays the lover: born of God. The verse displays the knowing: of God. That love is of God, and the one who loves is born of God, displays the divine origin and evidence of love: let us love one another, for love is of God — and everyone who loves is born of God and knows God; love is divine in source and the mark of the new birth. The verse displays the love of God, whose absence proves ignorance of God (v.8).
8:
"He that loveth not knoweth not God; for God is love." The brief verse notes the converse: the loveless do not know God, for God is love. The grammatical voices note the test and the great statement. Barnes and Robertson note the converse of v.7: "he that loveth not knoweth not (has no true knowledge of) God; for God is love" — the absence of love proves the absence of the knowledge of God; one who does not love cannot truly know God, because the very nature of God is love.

On "knoweth not God," the witnesses note the proof. Barnes and Calvin note that lovelessness is decisive evidence of not knowing God; since God is love, to be without love is to be without acquaintance with God; a loveless person, whatever his profession, does not know God.

On "God is love," the latin-fathers Augustine and the grammatical-historical note the supreme declaration: "God is love" — the sublime statement that love is the very essence and nature of God; not merely that God loves, but that God is love; love is what God is, so that to know God is to love, and to love is to share God's nature. The verse displays the converse: the loveless do not know God, for God is love.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the converse — "he that loveth not knoweth not God," lovelessness proving the absence of the knowledge of God (Barnes, Robertson); lovelessness decisive evidence of not knowing God, whatever the profession (Barnes, Calvin); "God is love," the supreme declaration that love is God's very nature and essence, not merely that he loves (Augustine, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the great statement. The verse marks the loveless: knows not God. The verse displays the proof: lovelessness. The verse displays the declaration: God is love. The verse displays the essence: love is God's nature. The teaching displays love as the very being of God. The verse displays the loveless: ignorant of God. The verse displays the God: love. The verse displays the knowing: through love. The converse — the loveless do not know God, for God is love — displays the heart of the chapter: the one who does not love does not know God, because God is love itself; love is the very nature of God, so that to be without love is to be without the knowledge of him. The verse displays the God who is love, manifested in the sending of his Son (v.9).
9:
"In this was manifested the love of God toward us, because that God sent his only begotten Son into the world, that we might live through him." The witnesses note the love of God manifested in sending his Son that we might live. On "in this was manifested the love of God toward us," the grammatical voices note the supreme display. Barnes and Robertson note the demonstration of God's love: "in this (by this act) was manifested (made visible, shown) the love of God toward us" — God's love is supremely revealed in a definite act; the sending of his Son is the visible manifestation of the love that God is (v.8).

On "God sent his only begotten Son into the world," the witnesses note the gift. Barnes and Calvin note the measure of the love: "God sent his only begotten (monogenes — unique, only) Son into the world" — the love is shown in the greatness of the gift: not a created being but his own only Son, sent into the world; the costliness of the gift measures the love.

On "that we might live through him," the grammatical-historical note the purpose: God sent his Son "that we might live (have spiritual and eternal life) through him" — the purpose of the sending was that we, dead in sin, might live through Christ; the love aimed at giving life to the spiritually dead; life through the Son is the goal of the love. The verse displays the love of God manifested in sending his Son that we might live.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the display — "in this was manifested the love of God toward us," God's love supremely revealed in a definite act (Barnes, Robertson); "God sent his only begotten Son into the world," the love shown in the greatness of the gift, his own only Son (Barnes, Calvin); "that we might live through him," the purpose to give life to the spiritually dead through Christ (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the manifested love. The verse marks the manifestation: God's love. The verse displays the gift: his only Son. The verse displays the sending: into the world. The verse displays the purpose: that we might live. The display shows God's love proven in the gift of his Son. The verse displays the love: manifested. The verse displays the Son: sent. The verse displays the life: through him. The love of God manifested in sending his Son that we might live displays the supreme demonstration of the love that God is: God's love was shown in this, that he sent his only begotten Son into the world, that we might live through him — the costly gift of the Son aimed at giving life to the dead. The verse displays the manifested love, defined not by our love but by his (v.10).
10:
"Herein is love, not that we loved God, but that he loved us, and sent his Son to be the propitiation for our sins." The witnesses note that love is defined by God's love for us in the propitiation. On "herein is love, not that we loved God, but that he loved us," the grammatical voices note the source of love. Barnes and Robertson note the definition: "herein is love, not that we (first) loved God, but that he loved us" — true love is defined not by our love for God but by his prior love for us; God's love is original, not a response to ours; he loved us first, when we did not love him.

On "not that we loved God, but that he loved us," the witnesses note the priority of God's love. Barnes and Calvin note that God's love precedes and causes ours; we did not earn or initiate it; his love for unworthy, unloving sinners is the pattern and source of all love; love begins with God.

On "sent his Son to be the propitiation for our sins," the latin-fathers Augustine and the grammatical-historical note the proof: God "sent his Son to be the propitiation (hilasmos — the atoning sacrifice that turns away wrath and covers sin) for our sins" — the proof of his love is the sending of his Son as the propitiation; Christ's atoning sacrifice for our sins is the supreme expression of the love that loved us first; love is shown in the cross that deals with sin. The verse displays that love is defined by God's love for us in the propitiation.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the definition — "herein is love, not that we loved God, but that he loved us," love defined by God's prior love, not ours (Barnes, Robertson); God's love preceding and causing ours, loving unworthy sinners first (Barnes, Calvin); "sent his Son to be the propitiation for our sins," the atoning sacrifice the proof of the love that loved us first (Augustine, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the defining love. The verse marks the source: not our love but his. The verse displays the priority: he loved us first. The verse displays the proof: the propitiation. The verse displays the sacrifice: for our sins. The teaching displays God's prior love proven in the atonement. The verse displays the love: God's. The verse displays the propitiation: his Son. The verse displays the sins: covered. That love is defined by God's love for us in the propitiation displays the true measure of love: love is not that we loved God, but that he loved us and sent his Son to be the propitiation for our sins — God's prior, original love, proven in the atoning sacrifice of his Son. The verse displays the propitiating love, which obliges us to love one another (v.11).
11:
"Beloved, if God so loved us, we ought also to love one another." The brief verse notes the obligation to love one another, since God so loved us. The grammatical voices note the inference. Barnes and Robertson note the duty drawn from God's love: "if God so loved us (so greatly, at such cost, in sending his Son), we ought also (are bound) to love one another" — God's great love for us lays an obligation on us to love one another; the love received obliges love given.

On "if God so loved us," the witnesses note the measure. Barnes and Calvin note that the "so" points back to the greatness of God's love just described (vv.9-10) — love at the cost of his Son, love to the unworthy; such love is the ground and measure of our obligation.

On "we ought also to love one another," the puritan Henry notes the duty: "we ought also to love one another" — the proper response to being so loved is to love our fellow-believers; the love of God received must flow out in love to others; not to love would be to despise the love we have received. The verse displays the obligation to love one another, since God so loved us.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the inference — "if God so loved us, we ought also to love one another," God's great love laying an obligation to love (Barnes, Robertson); the "so" pointing to the greatness of God's love at the cost of his Son (Barnes, Calvin); "we ought also to love one another," the love received flowing out in love to others (Henry). The verse displays the obligation. The verse marks the love: God's so great. The verse displays the duty: we ought. The verse displays the response: love one another. The verse displays the ground: God's love. The inference displays God's love obliging ours. The verse displays the loving: God's. The verse displays the obligation: ours. The verse displays the one another: loved. The obligation to love one another, since God so loved us, displays the practical duty flowing from God's love: if God so loved us — at the cost of his Son — we are bound also to love one another; the love received must flow out in love given. The verse displays the obligation of love, by which the unseen God is known (v.12).
12:
"No man hath seen God at any time. If we love one another, God dwelleth in us, and his love is perfected in us." The witnesses note that the unseen God dwells in us when we love, his love perfected. On "no man hath seen God at any time," the grammatical voices note the invisible God. Barnes and Robertson note the statement (echoing John 1:18): "no man hath seen God at any time (God in his essence is invisible, has never been seen)" — God himself is unseen; he is not perceived directly by human sight.

On "if we love one another, God dwelleth in us," the witnesses note the indwelling. Barnes and Calvin note the consequence: "if we love one another, God dwelleth (abides) in us" — though God is invisible, his presence is known and made real through love; when believers love one another, the unseen God dwells in them; love is the evidence and effect of his indwelling.

On "his love is perfected in us," the latin-fathers Augustine and the grammatical-historical note the completion: "his love is perfected (teleioutai — brought to completion, full expression) in us" — God's love reaches its goal and full expression in us when we love one another; the love God has shown us is completed and made perfect as it flows out in our love to others; mutual love is the perfecting of God's love in the believer. The verse displays that the unseen God dwells in us when we love, his love perfected.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the invisible God — "no man hath seen God at any time," God himself unseen, never directly perceived (John 1:18, Barnes, Robertson); "if we love one another, God dwelleth in us," the unseen God's presence made real through love, love the evidence of his indwelling (Barnes, Calvin); "his love is perfected in us," God's love reaching full expression as it flows out in our mutual love (Augustine, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the indwelling love. The verse marks the God: unseen. The verse displays the indwelling: God in us. The verse displays the condition: if we love. The verse displays the perfecting: his love completed. The teaching displays the unseen God made known through love. The verse displays the God: invisible. The verse displays the dwelling: in us. The verse displays the love: perfected. That the unseen God dwells in us when we love, his love perfected, displays the way the invisible God is made known: no one has ever seen God — but if we love one another, God dwells in us and his love is perfected in us; mutual love is the dwelling-place and the completion of the love of the unseen God. The verse displays the perfected love, witnessed by the indwelling Spirit (v.13).
13:
"Hereby know we that we dwell in him, and he in us, because he hath given us of his Spirit." The brief verse notes that the gift of the Spirit assures the mutual indwelling. The grammatical voices note the witness of the Spirit. Barnes and Robertson note the proof of the union: "hereby (by this) know we that we dwell in him, and he in us, because he hath given us of his Spirit" — the assurance of the mutual indwelling (we in God, God in us) rests on the gift of the Holy Spirit; the indwelling Spirit is the evidence and seal of the union with God.

On "we dwell in him, and he in us," the witnesses note the mutual abiding. Barnes and Calvin note the reciprocal indwelling (cf. 3:24): believer and God dwell in each other; this intimate communion is real and is known by the Spirit's presence.

On "because he hath given us of his Spirit," the grammatical-historical note the ground of assurance: "he hath given us of (a portion of, the gift of) his Spirit" — the giving of the Holy Spirit is the ground by which we know God dwells in us; the Spirit's presence and operation in the heart are the witness of the union; love (v.12) and the Spirit together attest the indwelling. The verse displays that the gift of the Spirit assures the mutual indwelling.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the witness — "hereby know we that we dwell in him, and he in us, because he hath given us of his Spirit," the gift of the Spirit the evidence of the mutual indwelling (Barnes, Robertson); the reciprocal abiding known by the Spirit's presence (3:24, Barnes, Calvin); "given us of his Spirit," the Spirit's presence the witness of the union (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the Spirit's witness. The verse marks the assurance: we dwell in him. The verse displays the indwelling: he in us. The verse displays the ground: his Spirit given. The verse displays the witness: the Spirit. The assurance displays the union attested by the indwelling Spirit. The verse displays the dwelling: mutual. The verse displays the Spirit: given. The verse displays the union: known. That the gift of the Spirit assures the mutual indwelling displays the ground of assurance: we know that we dwell in God and he in us because he has given us of his Spirit — the indwelling Spirit the witness and seal of the union. The verse displays the witnessing Spirit, joined to the testimony of the apostles (v.14).
14:
"And we have seen and do testify that the Father sent the Son to be the Saviour of the world." The witnesses note the apostolic testimony that the Father sent the Son as Saviour of the world. On "we have seen and do testify," the grammatical voices note the eyewitness witness. Barnes and Robertson note the apostolic affirmation: "we have seen (with our own eyes) and do testify (bear witness)" — the apostles, eyewitnesses of Christ, bear testimony to what they have seen; their witness is grounded in firsthand experience (cf. 1:1-3).

On "the Father sent the Son to be the Saviour of the world," the witnesses note the content of the testimony. Barnes and Calvin note the great truth witnessed: "the Father sent the Son to be the Saviour (deliverer, redeemer) of the world" — the apostles testify that God the Father sent his Son to save the world; the mission of the Son as Saviour is the substance of their witness.

On "the Saviour of the world," the grammatical-historical note the scope: the Son is "the Saviour of the world (kosmos)" — not of a narrow group only but of the world; the breadth of the title marks the fullness of the salvation Christ brings; the love of God reaches to the world in the sending of the Saviour. The verse displays the apostolic testimony that the Father sent the Son as Saviour of the world.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the eyewitness witness — "we have seen and do testify," the apostles bearing firsthand witness to Christ (1:1-3, Barnes, Robertson); "the Father sent the Son to be the Saviour of the world," the great truth witnessed, the Son's saving mission (Barnes, Calvin); "the Saviour of the world," the breadth of the salvation Christ brings (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the apostolic testimony. The verse marks the witness: seen and testified. The verse displays the content: the Father sent the Son. The verse displays the mission: Saviour. The verse displays the scope: of the world. The testimony displays the apostles witnessing Christ the Saviour. The verse displays the seeing: firsthand. The verse displays the Son: sent. The verse displays the Saviour: of the world. The apostolic testimony that the Father sent the Son as Saviour of the world displays the firsthand witness undergirding the message of love: we have seen and bear witness that the Father sent the Son to be the Saviour of the world — the apostles testifying to the saving mission that proves God's love. The verse displays the witnessed Saviour, confessed by those in whom God dwells (v.15).
15:
"Whosoever shall confess that Jesus is the Son of God, God dwelleth in him, and he in God." The brief verse notes that confessing Jesus as the Son of God brings the mutual indwelling. The grammatical voices note the confession. Barnes and Robertson note the test joined to indwelling: "whosoever shall confess (openly acknowledge, profess) that Jesus is the Son of God, God dwelleth in him, and he in God" — the sincere confession of Jesus as the Son of God is joined to the mutual indwelling; such confession (when genuine, from the heart) marks and accompanies the abiding of God in the believer.

On "confess that Jesus is the Son of God," the witnesses note the true confession. Barnes and Calvin note that this confession is not mere words but the heartfelt acknowledgment of Jesus as God's Son; the genuine confession springs from faith and is the mark of the indwelling God; it is the confession against which the false spirits stand (vv.2-3).

On "God dwelleth in him, and he in God," the grammatical-historical note the union: the one who truly confesses Christ has "God dwelling in him, and he in God" — the reciprocal indwelling, the same union spoken of throughout (v.13, 3:24); faith's confession and the mutual abiding go together. The verse displays that confessing Jesus as the Son of God brings the mutual indwelling.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the confession — "whosoever shall confess (acknowledge) that Jesus is the Son of God, God dwelleth in him, and he in God," sincere confession joined to the mutual indwelling (Barnes, Robertson); the confession not mere words but heartfelt faith, the mark of the indwelling God (Barnes, Calvin); "God dwelleth in him, and he in God," the reciprocal union accompanying faith's confession (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the confessing believer. The verse marks the confession: Jesus the Son of God. The verse displays the indwelling: God in him. The verse displays the union: he in God. The verse displays the mark: genuine faith. The confession displays the mutual abiding joined to confessing Christ. The verse displays the confessing: of the Son. The verse displays the dwelling: mutual. The verse displays the faith: genuine. That confessing Jesus as the Son of God brings the mutual indwelling displays the union joined to faith's confession: whoever sincerely confesses that Jesus is the Son of God has God dwelling in him and he in God — the heartfelt confession and the mutual abiding inseparable. The verse displays the confessing union, grounded in knowing and believing God's love (v.16).
16:
"And we have known and believed the love that God hath to us. God is love; and he that dwelleth in love dwelleth in God, and God in him." The witnesses note that dwelling in love is dwelling in God, who is love. On "we have known and believed the love that God hath to us," the grammatical voices note the experience of God's love. Barnes and Robertson note the believer's apprehension: "we have known (come to know) and believed (trusted, rested in) the love that God hath to (toward) us" — believers have both known and trusted God's love for them; knowledge and faith together lay hold of the love of God.

On "God is love," the witnesses note the repeated declaration. Barnes and Calvin note the central truth restated (v.8): "God is love" — the very nature of God is love; this is the foundation of all that John says about love; God's being is love itself.

On "he that dwelleth in love dwelleth in God, and God in him," the latin-fathers Augustine and the grammatical-historical note the union through love: "he that dwelleth (abides) in love dwelleth in God, and God in him" — since God is love, to abide in love is to abide in God; love is the element in which the believer and God dwell in each other; the one who lives in love lives in God, for love is God's own nature. The verse displays that dwelling in love is dwelling in God, who is love.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the experience — "we have known and believed the love that God hath to us," believers knowing and trusting God's love (Barnes, Robertson); "God is love," the central truth restated, God's nature love itself (v.8, Barnes, Calvin); "he that dwelleth in love dwelleth in God, and God in him," to abide in love being to abide in God, love the element of the union (Augustine, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the union of love. The verse marks the knowing: God's love. The verse displays the believing: trusted. The verse displays the declaration: God is love. The verse displays the union: dwelling in love and God. The teaching displays love as the element of communion with God. The verse displays the love: known. The verse displays the God: love. The verse displays the dwelling: mutual. That dwelling in love is dwelling in God, who is love, displays the heart of the chapter: we have known and trusted God's love; God is love, and the one who dwells in love dwells in God, and God in him — love being the very element in which the believer and God abide in each other. The verse displays the dwelling in love, which gives boldness in judgment (v.17).
17:
"Herein is our love made perfect, that we may have boldness in the day of judgment: because as he is, so are we in this world." The witnesses note love perfected, giving boldness in the day of judgment. On "herein is our love made perfect, that we may have boldness in the day of judgment," the grammatical voices note the fruit of perfected love. Barnes and Robertson note the confidence love brings: "herein is our love made perfect (brought to completion), that we may have boldness (parresia — confidence, freedom from dread) in the day of judgment" — love brought to maturity in the believer produces confidence for the judgment; the one in whom love is perfected can face the day of judgment without terror.

On "boldness in the day of judgment," the witnesses note the confidence. Barnes and Calvin note that perfected love casts out the dread of judgment; the believer in whom God's love dwells and is completed approaches the judgment with assurance, not fear; love is the ground of holy boldness before God.

On "because as he is, so are we in this world," the latin-fathers Augustine and the grammatical-historical note the likeness to Christ: the ground of the boldness is that "as he (Christ) is, so are we in this world" — the believer is made like Christ even now, in this present world; sharing Christ's character (especially his love), and standing in Christ's righteousness, the believer can face judgment confidently, for he is as Christ is. The verse displays love perfected, giving boldness in the day of judgment.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the fruit — "herein is our love made perfect, that we may have boldness (confidence) in the day of judgment," perfected love producing confidence for judgment (Barnes, Robertson); perfected love casting out the dread of judgment, the ground of holy boldness (Barnes, Calvin); "as he is, so are we in this world," the believer made like Christ now, standing in his likeness and righteousness (Augustine, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the bold love. The verse marks the love: made perfect. The verse displays the fruit: boldness. The verse displays the occasion: day of judgment. The verse displays the ground: as Christ, so we. The teaching displays perfected love giving confidence for judgment. The verse displays the love: perfected. The verse displays the boldness: held. The verse displays the likeness: to Christ. Love perfected, giving boldness in the day of judgment, displays the confidence that mature love brings: in this is our love made perfect, that we may have boldness in the day of judgment — because as Christ is, so are we in this world; the believer made like Christ faces the judgment without dread. The verse displays the bold love, which casts out fear (v.18).
18:
"There is no fear in love; but perfect love casteth out fear: because fear hath torment. He that feareth is not made perfect in love." The witnesses note that perfect love casts out the fear that has torment. On "there is no fear in love; but perfect love casteth out fear," the grammatical voices note the incompatibility. Barnes and Robertson note the principle: "there is no fear (dread, terror) in love; but perfect (mature, complete) love casteth out (drives out, expels) fear" — love and slavish fear cannot coexist; mature love expels the dread of God and of judgment; as love grows, fear departs.

On "because fear hath torment," the witnesses note the nature of fear. Barnes and Calvin note that "fear hath torment (kolasin — pain, anguish, punishment)" — the fear in view is the tormenting dread of punishment, the slavish fear that anticipates wrath; such fear is painful and is the opposite of the confidence love gives; it is the fear of the guilty before a judge.

On "he that feareth is not made perfect in love," the eastern Cyril and grammatical-historical note the test: "he that feareth (with this slavish dread) is not made perfect (matured, completed) in love" — the presence of tormenting fear shows that love has not yet reached maturity; the one still dominated by dread of punishment has not been perfected in love; the measure of love is the absence of slavish fear. (This does not exclude reverent, filial fear, but the slavish dread of wrath.) The verse displays that perfect love casts out the fear that has torment.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the incompatibility — "there is no fear in love; but perfect (mature) love casteth out fear," love and slavish fear cannot coexist, mature love expelling dread (Barnes, Robertson); "fear hath torment (anguish, punishment)," the tormenting dread of punishment, the slavish fear of the guilty (Barnes, Calvin); "he that feareth is not made perfect in love," tormenting fear showing love not yet matured, not excluding filial fear (Cyril, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the fear-expelling love. The verse marks the love: no fear in it. The verse displays the casting out: of fear. The verse displays the nature: fear has torment. The verse displays the test: fear shows imperfect love. The teaching displays mature love driving out slavish dread. The verse displays the love: perfect. The verse displays the fear: cast out. The verse displays the torment: of dread. That perfect love casts out the fear that has torment displays the liberating fruit of mature love: there is no fear in love, but perfect love drives out fear, because fear has torment — and the one still gripped by slavish dread has not been perfected in love. The verse displays the fear-casting love, which flows from God's prior love (v.19).
19:
"We love him, because he first loved us." The brief verse notes that our love springs from God's prior love. The grammatical voices note the source of love. Barnes and Robertson note the foundational truth: "we love him (or, we love), because he first (prior to and before us) loved us" — our love for God (and for the brethren) is the response to and the fruit of God's prior love; he loved us first, and our love is the echo of his; love begins with God.

On "he first loved us," the witnesses note the priority. Barnes and Calvin note that God's love is original and ours derivative; "he first loved us" means his love precedes, causes, and kindles ours; we could not love had he not first loved us; all love in the believer is the reflection of God's antecedent love.

On "we love him," the puritan Henry notes the response: "we love" — the believer's love, whether to God or to the brethren, is the grateful response to being first loved; the experience of God's love produces love in return; love answers love. The verse displays that our love springs from God's prior love.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the source — "we love him, because he first loved us," our love the response to and fruit of God's prior love, the echo of his (Barnes, Robertson); "he first loved us," God's love original and ours derivative, his preceding and kindling ours (Barnes, Calvin); "we love," the believer's love the grateful response to being first loved, love answering love (Henry). The verse displays the responsive love. The verse marks the loving: we love. The verse displays the cause: he first loved us. The verse displays the priority: God's love first. The verse displays the response: love answering love. The teaching displays our love as the fruit of God's prior love. The verse displays the love: ours. The verse displays the first: God's. The verse displays the response: kindled. That our love springs from God's prior love displays the root of all Christian love: we love because he first loved us — our love for God and the brethren the grateful echo of his antecedent love, which alone kindles ours. The verse displays the responsive love, which cannot coexist with hatred of the brother (v.20).
20:
"If a man say, I love God, and hateth his brother, he is a liar: for he that loveth not his brother whom he hath seen, how can he love God whom he hath not seen?" The witnesses note that love of God cannot coexist with hatred of the brother. On "if a man say, I love God, and hateth his brother, he is a liar," the grammatical voices note the contradiction. Barnes and Robertson note the exposure of false profession: "if a man say (claims), I love God, and hateth his brother, he is a liar" — to profess love for God while hating one's brother is a lie; the two are incompatible; the hater of his brother who claims to love God is self-contradicted.

On "he that loveth not his brother whom he hath seen," the witnesses note the visible test. Barnes and Calvin note the argument from the seen to the unseen: "he that loveth not his brother whom he hath seen (the visible fellow-believer)" — the brother is seen, near, and concrete; love for him is the testable, tangible thing; failure to love the visible brother betrays the absence of love.

On "how can he love God whom he hath not seen?" the latin-fathers Augustine and the grammatical-historical note the conclusion: "how can he love God whom he hath not seen?" — if one does not love the brother he can see, he cannot truly love the God he cannot see; love for the unseen God is proved by love for the seen brother; the visible love is the test of the invisible. The verse displays that love of God cannot coexist with hatred of the brother.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the contradiction — "if a man say, I love God, and hateth his brother, he is a liar," professing love for God while hating the brother a lie, the two incompatible (Barnes, Robertson); "he that loveth not his brother whom he hath seen," the visible brother the testable object of love (Barnes, Calvin); "how can he love God whom he hath not seen?" love for the seen brother the proof of love for the unseen God (Augustine, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the test of love. The verse marks the claim: I love God. The verse displays the contradiction: hating the brother. The verse displays the lie: exposed. The verse displays the argument: seen to unseen. The teaching displays love of God proved by love of the brother. The verse displays the lover: a liar. The verse displays the brother: seen. The verse displays the God: unseen. That love of God cannot coexist with hatred of the brother displays the searching test: the one who says "I love God" yet hates his brother is a liar — for if he does not love the brother he has seen, he cannot love the God he has not seen; love for the visible brother is the proof of love for the invisible God. The verse displays the test of brotherly love, bound by God's own commandment (v.21).
21:
"And this commandment have we from him, That he who loveth God love his brother also." The brief verse notes the commandment binding love of God to love of the brother. The grammatical voices note the divine command. Barnes and Robertson note the binding charge: "this commandment have we from him (from God, or Christ), That he who loveth God love his brother also" — God himself has commanded that love for him be joined to love for the brother; the two loves are bound together by divine command.

On "this commandment have we from him," the witnesses note the authority. Barnes and Calvin note that the joining of the two loves is not a human inference only but a command from God (cf. Mark 12:30-31); the authority of God binds love of God and love of neighbour together; they cannot be separated.

On "he who loveth God love his brother also," the puritan Henry notes the inseparable duty: "he who loveth God love his brother also" — the one who loves God is bound, by God's own command, to love his brother; love for God necessarily includes love for the brethren; the second flows from and proves the first. The verse displays the commandment binding love of God to love of the brother.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the command — "this commandment have we from him, That he who loveth God love his brother also," God commanding that love for him be joined to love for the brother (Barnes, Robertson); the joining a divine command, not human inference, binding the two loves (Mark 12:30-31, Barnes, Calvin); "he who loveth God love his brother also," love for God necessarily including love for the brethren (Henry). The verse displays the binding command. The verse marks the command: from him. The verse displays the love: of God. The verse displays the joining: love the brother also. The verse displays the authority: God's command. The commandment displays the two loves bound inseparably. The verse displays the commandment: divine. The verse displays the loving: God and brother. The verse displays the binding: together. The commandment binding love of God to love of the brother displays the close of the chapter's teaching on love: this is the commandment we have from God, that the one who loves God must love his brother also — the two loves bound inseparably by divine command. The verse displays the commandment of love, closing the chapter on testing the spirits and the God who is love.
